perturbation.original_text: Recipient will disclose only such information as it determines is legally required and will use commercially reasonable efforts to obtain confidential treatme nt for any Confidential Information that is so disclosed. Recipient is not required to seek or obtain a judicial determination establishing confidential treatment, but it will cooperated in any efforts to do so by Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient will disclose information as it believes is legally required and will attempt to obtain confidential treatment for any Confidential Information that is so disclosed. Recipient is not required to seek or obtain a judicial determination establishing confidential treatment, and cooperation with the Disclosing Party's efforts is optional.
perturbation.explanation: The modifications introduce ambiguity and weaken the obligation to protect confidential information. Replacing 'determines' with 'believes' introduces a subjective element. 'Attempt' weakens the effort required to obtain confidential treatment. Making cooperation with the Disclosing Party optional further undermines the protection of Confidential Information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The CPRA requires public agencies to justify withholding records. The modified text introduces ambiguity about the recipient's obligation to protect disclosed information and weakens the commitment to seek confidential treatment, potentially leading to improper disclosure of information that should be protected under CPRA exemptions. By weakening the 'reasonable efforts' to obtain confidential treatment, it is easier to argue that withholding certain records is not justified, therefore potentially leading to the agency inappropriately disclosing information.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Cal Cities Oh no! Sorry about that. Can’t seem to find that page. Let’s see if we can help you get to where you want to be. Home Advocacy Education and Events Get Involved News Resources About Us
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, which contradicts the CPRA's requirement for agencies to justify withholding records. The original text uses 'determines,' implying a reasoned decision, while the modified text uses 'believes,' introducing subjectivity. Additionally, 'commercially reasonable efforts' is weakened to 'attempt,' reducing the required effort. The scraped snippet 2 is unhelpful, but the Law Explanation specifically mentions that this weakens the 'reasonable efforts' to obtain confidential treatment. This could make it easier for an agency to argue against withholding records, potentially leading to inappropriate disclosure, which would violate the CPRA's implied intent to protect certain information from disclosure as outlined in the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall protect the disclosed Confidential Information by using the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care, to prevent the unauthorized use, disclosure, dissemination, or publication of the Confidential Information as Recipient uses to protect its own Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient should endeavor to protect the disclosed Confidential Information by using a degree of care similar to what it uses to protect its own Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'shall protect' to 'should endeavor to protect' weakens the obligation. Removing 'no less than a reasonable degree of care' makes the standard of care less defined and more subjective. This introduces ambiguity about the level of protection required.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA protects trade secrets from misappropriation. By weakening the standard of care required to protect Confidential Information, the modified text makes it more difficult to prove misappropriation because the 'reasonable' efforts to protect the information are now weakened and it creates an ambiguous standard of care that is very difficult to measure. If the recipient's own confidential information is poorly protected, this clause offers little protection under the CUTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall protect' to 'should endeavor to protect,' along with the removal of 'no less than a reasonable degree of care,' weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. The Law Explanation states that this weakening makes it more difficult to prove misappropriation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'misappropriation' in California Code, CIV 3426.1, as the acquisition or use of a trade secret by improper means, or the disclosure or use of a trade secret without consent by a person who knew or had reason to know that their knowledge was acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use. The original text clearly establishes a duty of care, whereas the changed text introduces ambiguity and weakens this duty, thereby contradicting the conditions under which misappropriation can be established under the CUTSA as described in the Law Explanation. The ambiguity around the standard of care directly impacts the enforceability of the confidentiality provisions under trade secret law.

perturbation.original_text: In no event shall either P arty be liable to the other for consequential, special, indirect, incidental, punitive or exemplary loss, damage , or expense relating to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Neither Party will typically be liable to the other for consequential, special, indirect, incidental, punitive or exemplary loss, damage, or expense relating to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Adding "typically" introduces ambiguity and opens the door for arguments that, under certain circumstances, such liability *could* exist, contradicting the original intent of a complete waiver of these types of damages. It implies exceptions exist without specifying them.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1668 generally invalidates contracts that exempt parties from responsibility for their own fraud, willful injury, or violation of law. While the original clause is a standard waiver of consequential damages, the addition of "typically" could be interpreted as an attempt to waive liability for certain types of wrongdoing, potentially conflicting with the restrictions of Section 1668. A court could interpret "typically" to mean that sometimes the party *is* liable, which may be seen as an improper waiver of liability for fraud, willful injury, etc.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1668. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. UNLAWFUL CONTRACTS [1667 - 1670.12] ( Title 4 enacted 1872. ) 1668. All contracts which have for their object, directly or indirectly, to exempt any one from responsibility for his own fraud, or willful injury to the person or property of another, or violation of law, whether willful or negligent, are against the policy of the law. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The addition of 'typically' weakens the original clause, which was a complete waiver of liability for consequential damages. California Civil Code § 1668 states that contracts cannot exempt anyone from responsibility for their own fraud, willful injury, or violation of law. The changed text introduces ambiguity, suggesting that under some circumstances, the party *could* be liable for damages that were previously waived, creating a loophole where liability for actions covered by § 1668 (fraud, willful injury) might be improperly waived depending on how 'typically' is interpreted. This falls under 'Ambiguity Legal Contradiction' where a legal obligation is introduced ambiguously, making it difficult to enforce under state or national law, potentially resulting in non-compliance.
